observations.recommendation: We recommended and reiterated that Management: 1) Require the Inventory Committee to conduct the actual physical count of all PPE in accordance with the MNGAS for LGUs, COA Circular No. 2015-008 and COA Circular No. 2020-006 to properly prepare and submit the prescribed RPCPPE and RPCLRN within the prescribed period; 2) Instruct the Property/Supply Officer-Designate, in lieu of the absence of the GSO, to prepare and maintain the Local Road Inventory Map, updated Property Cards and Property Acknowledgment Receipts; 3) Prepare and maintain, in relation to the local road networks of the LGU, LRNLC, including the regular property ledger cards, also as basis for reconciliation with the General Ledger (GL) account balances and with the property records; 4) Adjust the items erroneously recorded as PPE to the appropriate accounts; 5) Prepare a detailed lapsing schedule in computing the yearly depreciation expense and adjust the books for depreciation expenses erroneously computed and recognized in the previous years; 6) Require the Property/Supply Officer-Designate and Municipal Accountant to make a final reconciliation of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly on the disposition and derecognition of non-existing/missing PPE and adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.
observations.reason: Not Initial steps were already undertaken by the responsible officials for the conduct of the CY 2024 physical count.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: 9) Require the concerned officials to furnish the Audit Team copies of the MOA on all fertilizer loan granted to farmer-debtors; 10) Require the concerned officials to prepare an updated schedule and individual SLs of the subject loans receivables, indicating the individual borrowers and the amount due and demandable as of report date to substantiate the balance reported in the FS and facilitate effective monitoring of the balances of every account; 11) Provide the Treasury Office a copy of this schedule which will serve as their basis in the enforcement of collection of the farmer-debtor’s unpaid loans, if still workable; and 12) Revisit the MOA and consider writing-off the uncollected loans to farmers who have suffered loss due to force majeure events in accordance with the terms and conditions of the contract.
observations.managementAction: Only one copy of MOA was furnished to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: MAO was not able to submit the updated schedule which resulted in the non-preparation of corresponding SLs. Despite absence of schedule/SLs, the MTO was still continuously collecting payments from farmer-debtors who voluntarily paid their loan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: 49) Require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile, take up necessary adjusting entries and prepare SLs for the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account for accurate and reliable presentation in the FS and proper monitoring of balances; and 50) Refrain from charging against the LDRRMF those expenditures not pertaining to disaster risk reduction and management or not in accordance with the primary purpose for which the fund was established, otherwise, these transactions shall be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The subject account remained unreconciled. The same audit observations were still noted in the current year. Management was advised to refrain from charging non-DRRMF expenditures to LDRRMF.
observations.reason: The subject account remained unreconciled; inappropriate charges to LDRRMF continued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: 51) Prioritize SWM programs and projects and provide/allocate enough budget for their full implementation; and 52) Exert more and consistent effort and conduct regular monitoring of the implementation of the PPAs on solid waste management as mandated under RA No. 9003 and other relevant laws/ordinances within the Municipality’s jurisdiction and impose appropriate sanctions where necessary to ensure the effectiveness of the solid waste management programs and policies.
observations.managementAction: Due to lack of sufficient funds, full implementation of priority SWM programs and projects could not be done. Not all ordinances were strictly enforced or implemented. The SWM properties were still not yet used/utilized.
observations.reason: Lack of funds prevented full implementation; some ordinances not enforced; SWM properties not yet utilized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and reiterated the following to Management: 57) Require the Inventory Committee to conduct annual inventory-taking of all PPE and timely submit the RPCPPE for all PPEs together with the RPCLRN of the LGU within the prescribed period; 58) Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, being the Property Officer in lieu of the absence of the GSO, to prepare and maintain the Local Road Inventory Map and Property Cards for each type of properties as basis for reconciliation with the PPE accounts with the accounting records; 59) Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain, in relation to the local road networks of the LGU, LRNLC, including the regular property ledger cards, also as basis for reconciliation with the GL account balances and with the property records; 60) Instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately transfer the completed PPEs recorded in the TF to the GF and to effect the necessary adjusting entries for the prior years’ depreciation expense as well as the reclassification of the erroneous recording of assets and expenses as PPE to avoid overstatement of PPE and government equity accounts; 61) Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to make a final reconciliation of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly on the disposition and derecognition of non-existing/missing PPE and adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability; 62) Require the Inventory Committee to immediately request authority to dispose those identified unserviceable and obsolete PPE and drop from the books the book value thereof upon approval in accordance with existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations; and 63) Assess its existing organizational structure and consider the appointment of a permanent GSO to perform the duly designated duties and responsibilities effectively and efficiently.
observations.managementAction: An RPCPPE was submitted but it was only based on the list of additions to PPE for CY 2024. No Local Road Inventory Map and Property Cards were prepared by the Accounting Office. PPEs under the TF were transferred to the GF during CY 2023 but without details. No reconciliation between the accounting records and the property records was made. Obsolete and unserviceable PPEs were not disposed. No permanent GSO position was created and appointed.
observations.reason: No physical count conducted; some property acknowledgment cards issued for recent purchases but Local Road Inventory Map not prepared; no final reconciliation; no permanent GSO; unserviceable PPEs not disposed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require: 67) The Inventory Committee or the Supply Officer-Designate to prepare and submit the duly accomplished RPCI; 68) The Municipal Accountant to properly record the purchases of inventories in the appropriate inventory accounts and the consumption thereof as expense, upon receipt of SSMI of a given period, to ensure the proper monitoring, controlling and issuances of inventory items; 69) The Supply Officer-Designate and the Municipal Accountant to maintain updated Supplies Ledger Cards and Stock Cards required of them; and 70) Supply Officer-Designate and the Municipal Accountant to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records and make adjustments, when necessary, particularly the write-off of non-existing inventory items, to reflect the true balance of the Inventory and Expense Accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was not able to prepare and submit the RPCI as of December 31, 2024. The Municipal Accountant properly recorded purchases in appropriate inventory accounts for some items. Stock Ledger Cards were maintained by the Accounting Office. The Supply Officer-Designate and the Municipal Accountant did not complete all actions.
observations.reason: RPCI not prepared; some inventory purchases were recorded directly to expense in prior periods; stock ledgers partially maintained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to submit plausible explanation/justification on the deficiencies and discrepancies noted in the submitted documents and the lacking documentary requirements for further evaluation to arrive at a decision in audit.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials submitted explanations; management said adjustments would be made; some lacking documentary requirements were still not submitted. The BAC Secretariat explained that inconsistencies noted were typographical errors or due to legitimate changes but without supplemental/bid bulletins.
observations.reason: Some explanations provided; lacking documents still not submitted; inconsistencies unresolved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the concerned officials to: 75) Submit an explanation relative to the delay of the Supplier to deliver the construction and heavy equipment and the bank’s release of LGU’s loan and full payment to the Supplier despite non-delivery; and 76) Impose the appropriate amount of liquidated damages and collect the same from the Supplier, and/or, enforce the remedies available under RA No. 9184 and its IRR.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials submitted a letter dated June 8, 2023 explaining the reasons for delay. Municipal Mayor informed that there was a sudden increase in the dollar exchange rate during CY 2022 which prompted the supplier to request for the adjustment of the contract price but did not succeed; Management could not impose and require the Supplier to pay the liquidated damages due to the impact of the increase in dollar exchange rates.
observations.reason: Supplier cited foreign exchange impact; Management did not impose liquidated damages for equity reasons.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to submit the As-Built Plans prepared by the Contractor for the above completed mentioned fully paid completed infrastructure projects and the Municipal Accountant, henceforth, to ensure that As-Built Plan is attached as one of the supporting documents before effecting final payment of completed infrastructure project.
observations.managementAction: Only the subject CY 2022 infrastructure contracts were submitted to the Audit Team. The As-Built Plans for CY 2023 infrastructure projects were not submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: As-Built Plans were not attached to final payment for many projects; some CY2022 contracts submitted but As-Built Plans lacking.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the: 94) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure strict adherence with the reglementary period in the rendition of accounts with complete supporting documents and necessary monthly financial reports and journals; and 95) Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the guidelines in the preparation and submission of the JEV for non cash and other transactions not recorded in the CRJ, CKDJ and CDJ together with the corresponding documents supporting each JEV recorded in the GJ as prescribed under the MNGAS for LGUs to come up with a more detailed and reliable financial records and financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Delays in submission of CY 2024 collection, disbursement and other accounts were still noted. The Accounting Office has submitted the CY 2024 Financial Statements within the prescribed deadline. The JEVs and supporting documents for General Journals were not submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Some financial statements submitted on time, but JEVs and supporting documents for General Journal not submitted; delays continue for some accounts.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that Management: 96) Prioritize issuance of demand letter to all AOs and employees to immediately liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances; 97) File a request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, if warranted, to the Office of the Auditor; and 98) Continue to strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances and avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.
observations.managementAction: No demand letters were issued. No request for authority to write-off was filed. Management did not take action.
observations.reason: No demand letters issued; no request for authority to write-off filed; no management action taken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and ascertain the cause of the negative balance of the Due to BIR account and immediately draw correcting journal entries to adjust the said account. We further recommended that journal entries be properly evaluated, analyzed and reviewed to ensure that postings are in correct accounts and that balances are accurate.
observations.managementAction: The Due to BIR account still has abnormal balances.
observations.reason: No action taken; abnormal negative balance persists.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the fund for COVID-19 response be disbursed only for that purpose to effectively attain the objective of planned eliminating/mitigating the effect of the deadly disease. Likewise, the implementation of COVID-19 PPAs must only be made strictly in accordance with its authorized budget. Henceforth, all future procurements must be planned ahead through the Annual Procurement Plan (APP).
observations.managementAction: There was no APP prepared for all COVID-19 related procurements during CY 2022. Most COVID-19 expenses pertain to catering services for vaccination program, fuel consumption for transportation of patients and purchase of test kits.
observations.reason: No APP prepared for many COVID-19 procurements; some procurements not planned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: 101) Require the BAC to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR in the procurement process. Every detail of the noted procurement documents must be reviewed as to its correctness, particularly the details of the Approved Budget for the Contract and the dates of NOA, Contract, NTP and other vital documents supporting the procurement; 102) Impose Liquidated Damages on the project having negative slippage of one tenth of one per cent for every day of delay based on the unperformed portion of the contract; 103) Require the contractors to post warranty security for all completed and accepted projects; and 104) Henceforth, cancel the award of contract for those winning bidders who were unable to post Performance Security but entered into a contract within the allowable number days as provided in RA No. 9184 and its IRR.
observations.managementAction: The same audit observations were still noted in current year transactions. Management did not implement all recommendations. Some projects had no negative slippage noted in CY 2023; no warranty securities were posted.
observations.reason: Same issues persisted into current year; warranty securities not posted; some procurement irregularities remained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the annual LDRRMFIP be aligned with the municipality’s 6-Year Comprehensive-Integrated Disaster Plan. Only those PPAs which are embodied in the aforesaid comprehensive-integrated municipal plan must be given due course in the formulation of annual MDRRMFIP. Further, the LDRRMC must monitor the implementation of disaster related PPAs and evaluate the use of fund thereof to avoid disbursement of ineligible PPAs using the LDRRMF. Moreover, immediately implement all planned disaster-related PPAs for better disaster risk reduction and management.
observations.managementAction: Finding reiterated: The MDRRMFIP for current year only partially implemented; some ineligible PPAs noted.
observations.reason: Not all PPAs implemented; some ineligible disbursements occurred.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly adhere to laws and regulations in accounting and payment of obligation. Appropriation ordinance of the current year must only be disbursed for expenditures incurred on that year. Accordingly, the payment of various expenses in the total amount of ₱264,843.00 is required to be refunded immediately by officials and employees responsible for its authorization and payment pursuant to Section 351 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: There was no refund/s made. No prior year’s expenses were charged against the current year appropriation ordinance.
observations.reason: No refunds made; prior year expenses remained charged in some instances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: 113) Require the BAC, through its Secretariat, to submit the necessary documentary requirements to support the payments for honoraria. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant must ensure the completeness of supporting documents before certifying and processing any DV for payment; 114) Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that withholding taxes are deducted from payments made in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table, remit the same to the BIR within the prescribed period and reclassify the remaining amount of the proceeds from sale of bid documents to Trust Liabilities (Bid Docs); and 115) Refund the amount of ₱25,000.00 and stop the granting of honorarium to the HOPE.
observations.managementAction: The required documents remained not submitted. Taxes were still not withheld from payments of BAC honoraria. No refund was made.
observations.reason: Required supporting documents not submitted; withholding taxes not withheld; no refund made for HOPE honorarium.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop immediately the practice of including the payments of loan amortizations to private lending institution as deduction from the payroll and performing the collection and monitoring activities for the private bank. Officials and employees who availed loans from the private bank must personally settle their obligations directly with the bank. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant must prepare an adjusting entry to recognize the income of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: Management refrained from allowing the grant of loans to new borrowers from these private lending institutions. Loan amortizations from existing unpaid loans were continuously remitted to these private lending institutions.
observations.reason: Existing unpaid loan amortizations continued to be remitted; new loans not allowed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Create an Inventory Committee that will conduct a complete and actual
physical inventory of all LGU assets to validate their existence, condition
and proper classification in compliance with the abovementioned rules and
regulations. Likewise, the inventory process must be properly documented
and reconciled with RPCPPE and accounting records to address the
aforementioned discrepancies;
b. Require the Accounting Office to properly maintain the required PPELC
instead of relying solely on SL in compliance with Section 13 of the Manual
on the NGAS for LGUs. Likewise, the GSO must ensure that the AREs and
PCs are updated for all assets, not just on newly acquired items. A
reconciliation process must be institutionalized between the GSO and
Accounting Office to align property records with financial reports; and
c. Require both the GSO and Municipal Accountant to make a final
reconciliation of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of
PPE account balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-
006, particularly on the disposition and derecognition of non-
existing/missing PPE and adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up
with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and
accountability.

We recommended that Management require the:
a. GSO to promptly submit the required RPCI to the Office of the Auditor for
verification;
b. GSO to maintain and regularly update the SC for procured supplies and
materials. Likewise, GSO to maintain an updated RegSPI and to submit
weekly RSPI to the accounting unit, serving as the basis for preparing JEV;
and
c. GSO to submit the SSMI to the accounting unit as the primary basis for
recognizing the appropriate expense account.

We recommended that Management require the immediate deposit/remittance
with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) of the amount of
₱134,434.15. Henceforth, require all the Collecting Officers to remit their collections
intact and in full amount, daily, weekly or as soon as the amount of collection reaches
₱5,000.00 and enforce the requirement that all deposits be supported by the RCD
before remittance to the Municipal Treasurer to prevent discrepancies, including over-
deposits.

We recommended that Management:
a. Continue its commitment to faithfully remit trust collections, however, the
current administration still has to exert effort in reconciling and
establishing the unremitted trust collections from prior years’ transactions
and once established, accordingly remit the same to the respective
government agencies;
b. Prepare and maintain consolidated SLs on the composition of the balances
of Due to BIR for withheld taxes on compensation and government money
payments, and Dues to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth for premiums,
contributions and loan amortization, and other subsidiary accounts, as
needed;
c. Appropriately charge and collect from the suppliers/contractors/employees
to whom the over-remittance of withheld taxes, premiums or contributions
pertain; and
d. Henceforth, ensure the conduct of monthly reconciliation of remittances
against withheld amounts to prevent recurrence of unreconciled or
inaccurate remittance of balances.

We recommended that Management to:
a. Stop the practice of charging expenses that are not included in the Approved
Annual LDRRMFIP;
b. Ensure the timely and full implementation of all programmed DRRMF
PPAs and make sure that disbursement remain within the appropriated
amounts per activity as outlined in the approved LDRRMFIP;
c. Comply with the timelines set forth in the aforementioned COA Circular
for submitting the monthly Report of Sources and Utilization of
LDRRMF. Likewise, promptly submit the year-end MDRRMF Report of
Sources and Utilization for CY 2024 to facilitate the timely verification of
the accuracy and propriety of the implemented PPAs;
d. Immediately submit the lacking supporting documents as indicated in the
attached Annex F. Likewise, require the Accounting Office to refrain from
processing DVs without complete documentation and to faithfully adhere to
the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 on the use of alternative mode of
procurement, particularly Shopping; and
e. Require the MDRRMO to refrain from charging against the LDRRMF
those expenditures not pertaining to DRRM or not in accordance with the
primary purpose for which the fund was established, otherwise, these
transactions will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management direct its GAD Focal Person to prepare a
GPB that can be implemented within the year and implement properly all planned
GAD activities to ensure that all GAD gender issues raised during the year are
properly addressed.

We recommended Management to:
a. Immediately submit the lacking supporting documents as indicated in the
attached Annex G. Likewise, require the Accounting Office to refrain from
processing DVs without complete documentation; and
b. Advise the BAC to conduct regular reviews and monitoring of procurement
activities to ensure compliance with the procurement laws. Additionally, the
LGU needs to strengthen its Annual Procurement Plan to ensure
procurement is well-planned and executed with proper procedures.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to:
a. Submit all of the subsequently perfected LGU contract agreements,
together with their complete supporting documents, within five days
from the date of execution;
b. Ensure that the contracts and payments are duly supported with complete
documentary requirements pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 and
COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
c. Submit the lacking documents and justification on the noted deficiencies.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to ensure that prescribed
planning and budgeting tools are prepared, submitted and effectively utilized and the
timelines in the planning and budgeting process are strictly observed as prescribed
under DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the BAC and concerned procurement personnel to strictly comply
with the provisions of Section 52.1 and Annex “H” of the 2016 Revised IRR
of RA No. 9184. Specifically, ensure that Shopping is resorted to only for
goods that are considered ordinary or regular office supplies and
equipment, and only under the conditions prescribed therein;
b. Conduct meticulous procurement planning and proper determination of the
mode of procurement to be used which must be indicated in the Annual
Procurement Plan; and
c. Ensure completeness of documentary requirements for the respective
alternative mode of procurement.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct its BAC and its TWG to carefully scrutinize the Contractor’s
manpower and equipment on all the projects to be awarded and not on
individual project only in case the latter is the lowest bidder in several
projects which have the same or overlapping implementation period.
Likewise, require the contractors to augment its manpower and/or
equipment to be utilized in the projects awarded to them;
b. Direct the concerned personnel to immediately submit the required
missing documents, subject for auditorial and contract review, for
proper documentation and audit validation; and
c. Strictly comply with the posting and reporting requirements under
Section 5.0 of LBC No. 154 dated February 6, 2024 to ensure
transparency, accountability and timely assessment/monitoring in the
Release and Utilization of LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC projects.

We recommended that Management:
a. Review and evaluate the existing manpower resources to ensure adequate
staffing pattern that suit the present needs of the offices and create clear-
cut policies and guidelines to address the issue on the necessity and propriety
of hiring JOs/holders of COS;
b. Maximize the use of existing work force and resort to JO hiring only in the
exigency of the service and if it is not feasible for the LGU to hire said
services under a casual or contractual appointment;
c. Clearly define in the contract the nature of works or activities to be done by
the JOs/holders of COS;
d. Ensure that all personnel-related transactions, including the hiring,
documentation and payroll processing of casual and JO workers, be
centralized through or conducted in close coordination with the HRMO;
e. Require the reconciliation of payroll records with official HRMO reports
before any payments are processed, to ensure the accuracy and legitimacy
of payees; and
f. Require the submission of duly accomplished DTRs, quantified
accomplishment reports and definite proof of identification, to substantiate
actual work performed prior to the processing and disbursement of any
payments.

We reiterated that Management: 7) Require the Municipal Accountant to work back and exert extra effort in identifying cause/s of unreconciled difference and to prepare and regularly submit to the Auditor bank reconciliation statements for all bank accounts for our timely review/verification and to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to take up reconciling items or adjustments; and 8) Consider closing bank account/s which is/are deemed unnecessary or a duplication of an existing account.
observations.managementAction: Management has already initiated partial compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: MBRS were prepared for most of the bank accounts, but not for the LBP Cataingan and DBP Placer. The MT explained that they could not close the other GF bank account with DBP Placer because they have an existing loan with it. Their primary bank account used in the GF is with LBP Cataingan.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to: 13) Exert extra effort to prepare SLs and make a detailed review of the two reciprocal accounts in order to identify the cause of the unreconciled amounts and immediately effect the adjustments upon verification; 14) Determine if the purposes for which the transfers between funds are made have already been achieved or completed, hence must be liquidated and recorded in the separate books of accounts to present fairly the balances of the said accounts in the FS; and 15) Henceforth, conduct thorough review of the Journal Entry Voucher prepared by her staff pertaining to the Agency’s transactions using the above-mentioned reciprocal accounts to avoid the incurrence of differences between accounts.
observations.managementAction: Only current fund transfers between funds and the completion of the purpose were correctly recorded in the books. However, prior years’ intra-agency fund transfers remained subject to reconciliation.
observations.reason: Current fund transfers between accounts were already correctly taken up. Prior years' intra-agency fund transfers remained subject to reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require: 16) The Property/Supply Officer-Designate and other concerned Heads of Offices to immediately submit to the Accounting Office, copy furnished the Audit Team, the documents supporting the issuance, consumption or distribution of various inventories held for consumption and distribution; 17) The Municipal Accountant to properly record the purchase of inventories in the appropriate inventory accounts and the issuance, consumption and distribution thereof as expense upon receipt of RIS and SSMI of a given period and supported with a duly accomplished and fully accounted distribution list and proof of actual distribution together with other required documents; 18) The Inventory Committee to conduct the required physical count of all supplies and materials and submit the RPCI; and 19) The Accounting Office and the Property Office to maintain complete and updated Stock Ledger Cards and Stock Cards, respectively.
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented: The Property/Supply Officer-Designate and other concerned Heads of Offices submitted documents; the Municipal Accountant properly recorded purchases in appropriate inventory accounts; however, RPCI was not able to be prepared and submit by the Inventory Committee; Stock Ledger Cards were maintained by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee was not able to prepare and submit the RPCI as of December 31, 2024. Some stock cards/ledgers were prepared by the Accounting Office but not consistently maintained by Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: 20) Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit liquidation reports of outstanding fund transfers received from various agencies which purposes were already implemented/completed, return any unspent amount and henceforth, strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 4 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; 21) Reconcile the Due to NGAs account, prepare the required SLs of all fund transfers included in the balances of the account under each fund and take up the necessary correcting/adjusting entries in the books for the fair presentation of the said account in the FS; and 22) Institute the filing of a request to the Commission Audit for authority to write-off and/or adjust the dormant or non-moving accounts as prescribed in COA Circular Nos. 2016-005 and duly supported by the documents required in the said Circular.
observations.managementAction: The liquidation reports furnished to the Audit Team pertained to current year/recent fund transfers, not the prior years’ fund transfers still outstanding in the books. Reconciliation is ongoing. The Trial Balance and FS as of December 31, 2024 was with a schedule of subsidiary ledger balances, however, unreconciled subsidiary accounts were still noted. No request was made.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is ongoing; trial balance and FS include schedule of subsidiary ledger balances but unreconciled subsidiary accounts remain. No request for write-off was made.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that Management require the Municipal Accountant to: 23) Exhaust all means to analyze and reconcile Assets and Liabilities accounts with abnormal balances as of December 31, 2023 and accordingly make the necessary adjustment/s of the affected accounts; and 24) Strictly adhere to the prescribed policies and procedures in recording financial transactions as well as the careful review of accounting entries before approval of the same.
observations.managementAction: Management has already initiated partial compliance with the audit recommendations. Reconciliation is ongoing. The Trial Balance and FS as of December 31, 2024 was with a schedule of subsidiary ledger balances, however, balances with abnormal balances were still noted.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is ongoing; some abnormal balances remain though reduced from prior year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the following offices: 25) The Municipal Assessor’s Office to comply with prompt submission of assessment roll to the Treasury Office for newly assessed or reassessed properties before the 31st day of December each year; 26) The Municipal Treasurer’s Office to properly prepare the taxpayers’ index card showing relevant information on tax delinquencies of each taxpayer; 27) Regularly update the same upon receipt of the assessment roll and upon determination of tax due and collectible for the year; 28) Prepare the duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to be submitted to the Accounting Office; 29) Prepare the certified list of all RPT delinquencies to be submitted to the Sanggunian concerned with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection; and 30) The Municipal Accountant to observe due diligence in recording transactions by using the appropriate accounts under COA Circular No. 2015-009, following the accounting entries/procedures for RPT and SET transactions and to reconcile the affected accounts and effect the necessary adjusting/correcting entries to present fairly the balances in the FS.
observations.managementAction: The Assessment Roll was timely submitted by the Municipal Assessor to the MTO, copy furnished the Audit Team. Taxpayer’s index cards were updated sometimes but still incomplete and not made on a regular and timely basis. No certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year was submitted to the Accounting Office; however amount due and collectible for the year was submitted to the Accounting Office. No certified list of delinquencies was prepared. The Municipal Accountant to observe due diligence was implemented.
observations.reason: Some actions implemented (assessment roll submission); taxpayer index cards remain incomplete and irregularly updated; some required certified lists not prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the MSWDO to thoroughly conduct the assessment of clients requesting financial assistance out of the AICS program to: 31) Ascertain the eligibility of the client to request assistance on his/her behalf or for another beneficiary; 32) Ensure that the authority of the client to avail the assistance in behalf of another person/beneficiary is clearly established in the assessment form and supporting documents; 33) Ensure that the reasonable and allowed amount of assistance is granted; 34) Ensure that clients availing a particular assistance more than once may be granted only within the limit prescribed by the LGU’s guidelines in line with DSWD MC No. 24; 35) Ensure that the kind of assistance availed of is under the LGU’s AICS program and DSWD MC No. 24, otherwise, accordingly refer the clients to other assistance/services that may be accessed from other LGU resources or institutions/networks; and 36) Ensure the existence of adequate documentary requirements before the issuance of the Certificate of Eligibility.
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented: The MSWDO will ensure assessment, authority documentation, appropriate amounts, limits and documentation per DSWD MC No.24.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to: 37) Submit the subject CY 2023 contracts and their supporting documents and explain for the noted deficiencies in the submitted documents; 38) Require the posting of warranty security as one of the documentary requirements for final payment and ensure that the validity period is in accordance with Section 62.2.3 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and 39) Exercise strictest scrutiny in the preparation of bidding documents, conduct of procurement activities, especially the bid evaluation, in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other relevant procurement guidelines and supporting the transactions with complete documentary requirements pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented: The concerned officials submitted contracts and supporting documents; however, similar audit observations were still noted in the current year’s transactions.
observations.reason: Some required As-Built Plans and warranty securities still not posted or attached; similar observations persisted into current year.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Prioritize issuance of demand letter to all AOs and employees to immediately liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances;
File a request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, if warranted, to the Office of the Auditor; and
Continue to strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances and avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and ascertain the cause of the negative balance of the Due to BIR account and immediately draw correcting journal entries to adjust the said account. We further recommended that journal entries be properly evaluated, analyzed and reviewed to ensure that postings are in correct accounts and that balances are accurate.

We recommended that the fund for COVID-19 response be disbursed only for that purpose to effectively attain the objective of eliminating/mitigating the effect of the deadly disease.  Likewise, the implementation of COVID-19 PPAs must only be made strictly in accordance with its authorized budget. Henceforth, all future procurements must be planned ahead through the Annual Procurement Plan (APP).

We recommended that the annual LDRRMFIP be aligned with the municipality’s 6-Year Comprehensive-Integrated Disaster Plan.  Only those PPAs which are embodied in the aforesaid comprehensive-integrated municipal plan must be given due course in the formulation of annual MDRRMFIP.  Further, the LDRRMC must monitor the implementation of disaster related PPAs and evaluate the use of fund thereof to avoid disbursement of ineligible PPAs using the LDRRMF. Moreover, immediately implement all planned disaster-related PPAs for better disaster risk reduction and management.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to laws and regulations in accounting and payment of obligation.  Appropriation ordinance of the current year must only be disbursed for expenditures incurred on that year.  Accordingly, the payment of various expenses in the total amount of ₱264,843.00 is required to be refunded immediately by officials and employees responsible for its authorization and payment pursuant to Section 351 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly observe the provision of Section 343 of RA No. 7160 in the payment for catering services;
Ensure that catering services, if duly funded as part of PPAs of the LGU, must be made in modest amount and not exorbitant, unnecessary and excessive; and
Stop the practice of granting snacks and meals for regular meeting of departments, councils and committees, otherwise, charge the catering expenses to the representation allowances of LGU officials hosting the said activities.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the BAC, upon receipt of the PR, to ensure that only the relevant specifications and performance requirements of the goods/materials to be purchased are indicated in the PR and RFQ without indicating the brand names;
Refrain from lump-sum appropriations but instead, ensure that the Local Appropriation Ordinance contain specific and identified object of expenditure for each PPA; and
Stop the practice of splitting of contracts and strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 in all its procurements.

We recommended that Management:
Require the BAC, through its Secretariat, to submit the necessary documentary requirements to support the payments for honoraria. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant must ensure the completeness of supporting documents before certifying and processing any DV for payment;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that withholding taxes are deducted from payments made in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table,  remit the same to the BIR within the prescribed period and reclassify the remaining amount of the proceeds from sale of bid documents to Trust Liabilities (Bid Docs); and
Refund the amount of ₱25,000.00 and stop the granting of honorarium to the HOPE.

We recommended that Management stop immediately the practice of including the payments of loan amortizations to private lending institution as deduction from the payroll and performing the collection and monitoring activities for the private bank. Officials and employees who availed loans from the private bank must personally settle their obligations directly with the bank. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant must prepare an adjusting entry to recognize the income of the LGU.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the BAC Secretariat to submit all advance copies of perfected contracts, POs and their supporting documents to the Audit Team within the prescribed period; and
Instruct the Designated Supply Officer responsible for accepting deliveries to:  i) notify COA of the time and date of the scheduled deliveries for ocular verification of the existence and condition of supplies, materials and equipment; ii) submit copies of completely filled-out AIRs within 24 hours upon acceptance; and iii) submit report on deliveries received, issued to end-users and remaining balance as of December 31, 2021.
